explanation_md: Without `std::boolalpha`, `std::cout` prints `true` as `1` and `false` as `0`. After `std::boolalpha` is activated, the same values print as the words "true" and "false". The manipulator stays active for subsequent output until `std::noboolalpha` is used.
`x == y` is `7 == 7` which is `true`. `x > y` is `7 > 7` which is `false` (equal is not greater). Both are stored in `bool` variables and printed with `std::boolalpha`, so they appear as words.

prompt_md: What does `std::cout << myBool` print when `myBool` is `true` and `std::boolalpha` has NOT been set?
explanation_md: Without `std::boolalpha`, C++ prints boolean values as integers: `true` becomes `1` and `false` becomes `0`. To get the text "true" or "false", you must output `std::boolalpha` first. Options c and d are never printed by the standard stream.

prompt_md:
Which of the following initializations is valid in C++ and sets `b` to `true`?

```cpp
bool b { 5 };
```

options.a: It is invalid; brace-initialization rejects non-bool values with an error.
options.b: It is valid; any non-zero integer converts to true.
options.c: It is valid; b stores the value 5, not true.
options.d: It is invalid; only 0 and 1 are valid bool initializers.

explanation_md: In C++, any non-zero integer value converts to `true` when assigned to a `bool`. The value is not stored as `5`; internally `bool` normalises it to `1` (true). Brace-initialization may produce a narrowing conversion warning but the code compiles and the result is `true`, not 5.
`p && q` is `true && false` = `false`. `p || q` is `true || false` = `true`. `!p` is `!true` = `false`. All three are printed with `std::boolalpha` so they appear as words.
